results.title: This Dating App Swaps Endless Chats With in-Person Dates
results.author: Sammi Caramela
highlights:
This Dating App Swaps Endless Chats With in-Person Dates
[...]
A recent survey by dating app Breeze found that nearly 9 out of 10 people feel burnt out from dating app conversations. Additionally, 90 percent said they’d rather skip messaging altogether and go straight to an in-person first date.
[...]
Realistically, most dating apps do not set us up for success. According to the survey, the average NYC single spends 8 hours on dating apps per week. An entire work day swiping through strangers.
[...]
That’s why, when I heard about Breeze, I was intrigued. This dating app operates without a chat function, instead pushing users to actually meet their matches in person.
[...]
While using Breeze, you only get a limited number of curated daily matches.
[...]
“Users immediately share their availability and schedule their date after matching at one of the Breeze-partnered bars and restaurants,” Breeze’s press release reads.
[...]
“Since its NYC debut in early 2025, Breeze has seen 32 percent month-over-month growth, emphasizing the desire for online dating to transition offline,” the release continues. “The platform now has over one million downloads, nearly 200,000 monthly active users worldwide, and almost 400,000 dates facilitated.”
[...]
The unique dating app coordinates reservations at one of its 800+ partner venues. In New York City, such venues include The Seville, Romeo’s, Zizi Wine Bar, and Juliette Restaurant.
[...]
“People are tired endless swiping, ghosting, excessive ‘premium’ fees, the gamification of your lovelife and, most of all, a neverending wave of boring, very horny or outright creepy messages,” said Marco van der Woude, Co-Founder and Head of Business Development at Breeze. “We built Breeze to help singles cut the noise and actually meet safely and intentionally.”

Tagged: Dating, First Dates, single life

results.title: Taking Online Dates Offline: Dating App Breeze Launches in the U.S., Starting with NYC
highlights:
The successful European platform aims to bring singles together IRL in the city with one of the highest amounts of dating app users in the world.
[...]
NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- Breeze, the dating app for real dates, announces its expansion into the United States. Starting with New York City, the platform targets online dating fatigue by encouraging users to schedule an in-person date right away without endless swiping and chatting.
[...]
With established success in Europe since its launch in 2020, Breeze has organized over 300,000 dates across the Netherlands, Belgium, Germany, the U.K., and France, with partner locations in more than 34 cities. Designed for all singles, the platform has also focused on establishing itself as an inclusive option for the LGBTQ+ community.
[...]
- No Chatting: Unlike other dating apps, Breeze does not have a chat function, meaning users take time back from hours of endless swiping. With limited curated potential daily matches, users immediately share their availability and schedule their date after matching at one of the Breeze-partnered bars and restaurants.
[...]
- Designed for Safety: All daters are screened and urged to verify their identity. First dates are hosted in safe and secure partner locations where the staff can keep an eye out for you.
[...]
- Intentional Dates: Breeze daters show their commitment to meeting each other in-person by paying up front. Users' accounts get frozen if they cancel too often. No-shows rarely happen, but if they do, users get banned from the app.
[...]
”We’re so excited to introduce New Yorkers to a new way of online dating by focusing on what we all crave—safe and face-to-face dates based on real chemistry,” said Marco van der Woude, Co-Founder & Head of Business Development of Breeze. “The average dating app user spends around 38 hours swiping and chatting before they end up meeting someone in real life, and it's clearly not working. We’re redefining the experience by eliminating pre-date messaging altogether, prioritizing face-to-face interactions.”
[...]
Breeze was founded in 2020 by seven student co-founders in the Netherlands with a mission to make online dating human again. Frustrated by the inefficiency of traditional dating apps, they set out to create a platform that prioritizes quality over quantity, making dating effortless and intentional. Breeze eliminates endless scrolling–removing the digital barriers that keep people from meeting in real life– focusing instead on effortless, real-life dates. Since its launch, Breeze has expanded across the Netherlands, Belgium, Germany, the U.K., France, and now the U.S., helping singles find genuine connection beyond the screen. Learn more at breeze.social.

results.title: Dutch Dating App Breeze Launches in the UK With Offline-First Model - Global Dating Insights
results.author: Staff Writer
highlights:
Dutch Dating App Breeze Launches in the UK With Offline-First Model - Global Dating Insights

7th January 2026 Staff Writer

Dutch dating app Breeze has officially launched in the UK, positioning itself as an alternative to swipe-heavy platforms by encouraging users to move quickly from matching to meeting in person. The rollout is supported by a new brand campaign that directly challenges what Breeze describes as the industry’s overreliance on extended in-app conversations.

The UK launch is accompanied by a national advertising push titled Love Is Worth The Leap, developed by creative agency Cloudfactory. The campaign centers on the anxiety many users feel before meeting a match face to face, reframing that hesitation as a necessary step toward meaningful connection. In the lead television spot, a woman receives a date notification that visually transforms her bedroom into a high diving platform, symbolizing the emotional leap involved in meeting someone without prolonged online chatting.

Breeze’s approach differs from most mainstream dating apps by limiting pre-date messaging. Instead of extended conversations, users match and are prompted to meet directly at a public venue arranged through the app. The company argues this reduces ghosting, endless swiping, and mismatched expectations that can develop through text-based interactions.

Sarah Fronckevic, chief marketing officer at Breeze, said: “Dating apps have become endless chat machines that keep people stuck behind screens. We built Breeze to do just the opposite. You skip the chat and go on a real date. We invite Breeze users to step out of their comfort zone, because real connection is worth it. We’re proud to work with Cloudfactory to bring this insight to life as we roll out our service across the UK.”

results.title: Sitch's new dating app fuses human matchmaking and AI | TechCrunch
results.author: Ivan Mehta
highlights:
Can AI improve the dating app market? A new dating app called Sitch aims to find out by leveraging human expertise in matchmaking to power its AI model.
[...]
Sitch aims to take a more thoughtful approach with its onboarding process and uses large language models (LLMs) to bring a human matchmaker’s expertise to the dating app experience, helping people find potential matches without swiping.
[...]
was co-founded by Nandini Mullaji, whose knack for the dating market comes from her grandmother, also a matchmaker. She said that while companies like Match and Bumble dominate the space, people are still unsatisfied and not getting the matches they want.
[...]
Mullaji noted that, up until recently — when LLMs became more commonly available — it was hard to scale the human-in-the-loop experience of matchmaking. DePue added that the startup’s goal is to extract data from profiles and pictures using LLMs and show personalized matches.
[...]
Essentially, Sitch built an AI version of Mullaji that helps users onboard by asking them details using almost 50 questions, which they can answer through text or voice.
[...]
After the dater’s profile is set up, the AI matchmaker displays its suggested matches. If both users agree to match with each other, the bot adds them to a group chat with the AI. At any point in time — even after their real-life dates — users can provide feedback about their matches to improve the AI’s personalization.
[...]
The startup initially trained its AI models with more than 75 parameters for matchmaking provided by Mullaji, and tapped into her matchmaking skills to understand why she thought two people would match up. Then the company used feedback from the users to scale its models.
[...]
match), and
[...]
Sitch is backed by $5 million in seed funding from M13 and a16z speedrun, with the startup getting $7 million in total funding to date. Anna Barber, a partner at M13, said that the venture firm was excited by the vision of a startup using AI to build a dating app.
[...]
Sitch currently offers its services just in New York but plans to open up shop in more cities this year. The company said it’s checking all profiles manually to maintain quality and safety.
[...]
The startup may have its work cut out for it because larger companies, like Tinder, Bumble, and Grindr, are also infusing AI into different parts of their app experiences.
[...]
Sitch is banking on the fact that people might be looking to move away from swipe-based apps, as the likes of Tinder and Bumble registered a slowdown last year. Plus, the company is confident that as users give the app more data and are intent on serious dating, it will stand out.
[...]
The app is currently available on Apple’s App Store.

results.title: Bumble introduces an AI dating assistant, 'Bee' | TechCrunch
results.author: Sarah Perez
highlights:
Dating app maker Bumble is venturing into generative AI. During the company’s fourth-quarter earnings on Wednesday, Bumble introduced a new AI assistant it’s calling “Bee,” designed to become a personal matchmaker that learns users’ “values, relationship goals, communication style, lifestyle, and dating intentions” through private chats. It then uses those insights to help find the user more relevant matches.

With Bee, the company envisions being able to capture much more information about Bumble users, as it learns more about each individual’s story and what they really want. Initially, Bee will be used to power a new dating experience called “Dates” that uses AI to recommend matches, but in the future, Bumble says Bee will move into other areas, like offering date suggestions or requesting anonymous feedback from your prior matches.

In “Dates,” Bee will first learn about the user through a private, onboarding conversation. It then identifies two people who have shared intentions, values, and relationship goals. Both users are notified in the app with a description of why they make a great match.

In fact, Herd said that Bumble would experiment with removing the long-popular swipe mechanism in select markets to see how users react. Instead of prioritizing swipes as a binary “yes” or “no,” Bumble is looking to leverage other features, like new “chapter-based” profiles where members can connect with one another on different parts of a user’s life story. results.title: Bumble’s AI assistant Bee will learn what you want from a relationship
results.author: Allison Steffens Herrera
highlights:
The dating app unveiled Bee at its Q4 earnings alongside a broader ‘Bumble 2.0’ overhaul, as the company attempts to reverse years of declining user numbers by replacing gamified swiping with AI-driven compatibility.
[...]
There is a version of the future, Bumble’s version, at least, where you never swipe on a dating app again. Instead, you have a private conversation with an AI that learns what you actually want from a relationship, sits quietly in the background, and surfaces one carefully chosen match with a note explaining exactly why the two of you belong together.
[...]
That is the pitch behind Bee, the AI dating assistant Bumble reveled at its fourth-quarter earnings on 11 March 2026.
[...]
The product is still in internal testing, with a public beta coming “soon” according to founder and chief executive Whitney Wolfe Herd, who described it to investors as a personal matchmaker that learns users’ values, relationship goals, communication style, lifestyle, and dating intentions through private chats.
[...]
Once Bee identifies two people it considers compatible, both are notified in the app with a summary of why they make a strong match. From there, the conversation and the date are up to the humans.
[...]
The launch vehicle for Bee is a new in-app experience called Dates. Users opt in, complete an onboarding conversation with Bee via text or voice, and then wait for a curated introduction rather than scrolling through an open pool of profiles.
[...]
Bee is the headline act in a broader product reset, Bumble is calling Bumble 2.0. Alongside the AI assistant, the company is experimenting with removing the traditional left-right swipe mechanic entirely in select markets, replacing it with what it calls chapter-based profiles, structured layouts that let users showcase different dimensions of their lives, from work and hobbies to values and plans.
[...]
JPMorgan upgraded
[...]
Bumble is not alone in the AI pivot. Tinder’s Chemistry feature uses personal questions and camera roll access to sharpen match recommendations. Grindr’s Edge subscription tier offers AI summaries of past chats and compatibility statistics.
[...]
What Bee represents, if it works, is a more ambitious commitment: not augmenting the swipe, but replacing it as the primary discovery mechanism.
[...]
The chapter-based profiles and potential no-swipe markets are scheduled for the second half of 2026, according to the product timeline Wolfe Herd shared with investors.
[...]
Bee’s beta will arrive before that. For a company that has spent most of the past two years shrinking, both in ambition and headcount, it is a notably bold sequence of bets all riding on the idea that what daters actually want is less choice, not more.

results.title: The Anti-Tinder Arbitrage
highlights:
Between May 2023 and May 2024, Tinder lost 594,000 users in the UK. Hinge shed 131,000. Bumble dropped 368,000. By Q2 2025, Bumble's paying users had fallen 8.7% to 3.8 million. Match Group—owner of both Tinder and Hinge—just reported its eighth consecutive quarter of declining payers.
[...]
Meanwhile, attendance at in-person dating events surged 42% between 2022 and 2023. Eventbrite logged 1.5 million searches for dating events in a 12-month window, with 376,000 attendees and an average of 128,000 monthly searches. Game-based dating events grew 88-163% depending on format. Athletic dating events—pickleball and spin classes for singles—jumped 136%. Thursday, a dating event company that hosts weekly singles mixers, now operates in 75 cities globally and regularly fills venues with 200-300 people per event.
[...]
grew up fully online
[...]
base is over
[...]
Gen Z globally
[...]
The apps know this. Bumble launched Bumble IRL in 2022—hosting spin classes, community service outings, and mixers. Match Group added a "72 Hours" feature designed to "disrupt the incessant messaging" and get people offline faster. Even dating apps are admitting that staying on the app is the problem.
[...]
Thursday Dating raised $3.5 million in seed funding months after launching in London and New York in May 2021. The company generated 100,000 sign-ups and 7,000 matches on launch day. Their model: the app only works on Thursdays. Users have 24 hours to match, chat, and meet up. The artificial scarcity creates urgency. But the real business is the events—weekly singles mixers in 75 cities with themes ranging from "Holiday Pairing Party" to "Line Dancing Hoedown" to "Singles Movie Night." Events regularly draw 200-300 people at $15-30 per ticket.
[...]
Misaligned incentives are obvious. Dating apps make money when you stay single. Match Group's former CEO literally said their goal is "monetizing loneliness." Hinge calls itself "the app designed to be deleted," but their parent company's stock price depends on people not deleting it. Even Hinge's own features confess the problem: they delete matches after 24 hours, limit daily matches unless you pay, and push IRL events. When your differentiation is "we don't want you to use us that much," the category is broken.
[...]
The supply side is fragmented and amateur. Most IRL dating events are run by individual operators using Gmail + Google Sheets + Eventbrite, venue owners throwing "singles night" as filler for slow weekdays, or matchmakers charging $5,000-$50,000 for 1:1 services. There's no Shopify for matchmakers. No Stripe for dating events. No infrastructure layer that lets anyone spin up a high-quality, repeatable dating club in their city.

results.title: From strangers to lovers: How this startup is simulating the ‘meet-cute’ with AI – DNYUZ
highlights:
222, a startup focused on relationship building with the help of AI, thinks it can bring back the spontaneity of making a new friend — or falling in love.
[...]
At a high level, 222 matches people with strangers for experiences like dinner or a night out after they take a robust personality quiz, using machine learning models trained by its team and open-source AI models.
[...]
222 launched its app in 2024 and has since added more features.
[...]
When 222 launched in 2021, it began as a dinner series in Los Angeles for young adults emerging from the COVID-19 pandemic, helping them meet new people. Then the project grew into a company. It was accepted into Y Combinator, raised capital, moved to New York City, and launched a mobile app to spur in-real-life (IRL) socializing.
[...]
Since putting out its app in 2024, the 222 experience has evolved. It’s no longer just about meeting strangers, having a fun night, and forming new relationships.
[...]
The platform is now digging deeper into connecting people after the first encounter that 222 initiates. It’s helping plan follow-up hangs with friends and kindling a romantic connection by setting people up on a date if the feeling is mutual.
[...]
After a 222 experience, the platform follows up to ask attendees whether they want to hang out or go on a date with anyone they met.
[...]
The startup knows its first pairings may not be the ultimate match, which is why it encourages its subscribers — who pay $22 a month — to try multiple experiences. Its AI, in return, can curate better matches from 222’s network.
[...]
Several startups have launched with this premise and are raising millions, pitching matchmaking solutions that use AI to set people up. Meanwhile, Bumble, Tinder, and Facebook Dating are testing the AI waters and reimagining the swipe. Hinge’s founder recently left the Match Group-owned dating app to build an AI dating alternative.
[...]
After raising another $10.1 million from venture capital investors in 2025 — bringing the startup’s total raised to $13.7 million — 222 is doubling down on hiring and expanding its product with tools that keep relationships going.
[...]
222’s next undertaking is to provide avenues for its users to reach their “next offline moment” together, so they can deepen those relationships.
[...]
“They need to show up at the same place together,” Kazemian said, be it a hangout, a date, or a restaurant reservation. “We can help them figure out what that place is.”

results.title: Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding for U.S. Expansion
results.author: Dennis Schaal
highlights:
Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding for U.S. Expansion
[...]
Airbnb has struggled to scale experiences. Investing in WeRoad — and hiring its CEO — is Chesky's preferred move: partner first, potentially acquire later.
[...]
Airbnb led a $58 million Series C funding round for European tour and experience platform WeRoad Wednesday — a day after announcing it had hired its CEO to lead Airbnb hotels.
[...]
With the deal and partnership, Airbnb gets a 10% stake in WeRoad and a board seat alongside its new VP of hotels, Andrea D’Amico. In addition to serving as WeRoad CEO since 2022, D’Amico worked as a top Booking.com hotel executive in EMEA for 18 years.
[...]
WeRoad, which operates multiday tours and has a platform for tour guides operating experiences, could help Airbnb scale its experiences business.
[...]
One of Airbnb’s biggest challenges in trying to scale its experiences business has been attracting quality tour guides to run them in a way that’s compatible with the Airbnb brand. There are no publicly laid out plans yet for Airbnb to tap into WeRoad’s tour guide network, to learn from them, or even to train them, but it’s clear that WeRoad wants to make the most of this marriage.

$70 million
[...]
WeRoad, which was founded in Italy in 2017, plans to use the proceeds from the funding to expand into the U.S. De Nadai said the company will hire a U.S. general manager, adding that he sees U.S. expansion as easier than expanding in some European markets like Portugal where the company would have to start from scratch.
[...]
WeRoad serves as a tour operator in popular destinations, and distributes tours from other vendors in less popular markets, De Nadai said. Its WeMeet app enables the WeRoad community to self-organize everything from a group trip to a Japanese restaurant to a Yoga lesson.
[...]
It’s the producers of those more organized experiences that might be attractive to Airbnb in helping to scale its experiences business.
[...]
With the Airbnb-led round, WeRoad has raised around $100 million to date. WeRoad has not yet named a successor to D’Amico.

results.title:
What WeRoad's $58M Series C Signals for Social Group Adventure Travel
 – New Market Pitch
highlights:
On May 27, 2026, WeRoad announced a $58M Series C led by Airbnb, taking total funding to about $100M. The money supports WeRoad's first major expansion outside Europe, with a U.S. launch built around existing and U.S.-specific itineraries, WeMeet local events, and U.S. Group Leader recruitment starting in Austin. The company says it has reached 300,000 lifetime travelers, including 100,000 in the last year, supported by 4,000 coordinators, 3.5M followers, and more than 1,000 itineraries.
[...]
The thesis is not simply that travel demand is back. The sharper thesis is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. WeRoad is betting that younger travelers, especially Millennials and Gen Z, are buying belonging, age-matched groups, social safety, and real-world connection alongside the destination itself. WeMeet makes that thesis more interesting because its 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 app downloads give WeRoad a local community wedge before the customer buys a trip.
[...]
100M
[...]
Adventures has about $14
[...]
Pack has about $
[...]
Current funding activity in community-led group adventure travel is thin in deal count but extremely concentrated in capital. Over the last 24 months, three category-relevant disclosed rounds stand out: WeRoad’s $58M Series C, WanderOn’s ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, Series A, and Much Better Adventures’ £5M, roughly $6.37M, growth investment.
[...]
WanderOn is the closest recent comparable. It raised ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, in a Series A on January 20, 2026. Its thesis is community-led, experience-first travel for Gen Z and Millennial travelers, with capital going toward global expansion, new experience-led formats, technology, and community. That is very close to WeRoad’s logic: younger customers, curated travel, group experience, and community beyond the trip.
[...]
The strongest evidence for the thesis is still company-specific. WeRoad has 300,000 lifetime travelers, 100,000 travelers in the last year, a 60% rebooking rate, one-third of new customers from word of mouth, 4,000 coordinators, and WeMeet metrics of 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 downloads. Those numbers show a company building both travel supply and community demand.
[...]
The retained investment thesis behind WeRoad's Series C is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. This thesis was retained because the round is explicitly tied to U.S. expansion, WeMeet local events, group leader recruitment, and WeRoad's reported traction across travelers, rebooking, word of mouth, coordinators, followers, itineraries, and event participation.

results.title: Airbnb Backs WeRoad To Deepen Group Travel And Community Ties - Simply Wall St News
highlights:
- Airbnb led a $58 million Series C funding round for European group travel startup WeRoad.
- The deal supports WeRoad's planned U.S. launch and expansion of community-focused, in-person events.
- The move extends Airbnb's reach beyond home rentals into group travel and offline travel communities.
[...]
This investment positions Airbnb more directly in group travel, a segment that connects online platforms with offline social experiences. For investors tracking its broader travel ecosystem, the WeRoad deal adds another piece to how the company may seek to deepen engagement with travelers beyond core short term stays.
[...]
For you as an investor, Airbnb leading WeRoad’s US$58m Series C round looks like a targeted way to extend its “whole trip” ambitions into group travel without building a new product from scratch. WeRoad’s focus on hosted group trips and in person community events sits close to Airbnb’s push into experiences and services, so this backing can be seen as an attempt to stay closer to younger, experience seeking travelers who often plan trips around social activities, not just accommodation. Compared with larger online travel competitors such as Booking Holdings, Expedia Group and Trip.com, this move leans more toward community and offline meetups, which could support engagement even when guests are not actively booking stays on Airbnb.
[...]
- ⚠️ Airbnb is putting more weight on newer travel formats, so if group travel demand or community events soften, the return on this capital could end up below expectations.
[...]
- ⚠️ Deeper involvement in curated trips exposes Airbnb more directly to operational, regulatory and liability issues around hosted tours and group experiences, on top of existing short term rental scrutiny.
[...]
- 🎁 If WeRoad’s US launch gains traction, Airbnb could get an early look at group travel trends and traveler behavior, which may inform product decisions across its broader platform.
[...]
- 🎁 The partnership provides another
[...]
From here, you may want to track how often Airbnb references WeRoad in future product updates, whether any integration appears in the Airbnb app, and how quickly WeRoad expands beyond its initial U.S. city launch. Signals around booking volumes, repeat trips and cross usage between WeRoad groups and Airbnb stays could show whether this partnership helps Airbnb deepen traveler relationships or remains a financial investment. Competitive reactions from Booking Holdings, Expedia Group and other travel platforms in group tours and community led travel will also help you judge how differentiated this move really is.

results.title: WeRoad Secures $58M Investment from Airbnb to Improve Its U.S. Group Travel Platform - Tech Weekly
results.author: Lauren Schultz
highlights:
WeRoad Secures $58M Investment from Airbnb to Improve Its U.S. Group Travel Platform - Tech Weekly
[...]
WeRoad, a Milan-based startup focused on group travel, has secured an impressive $58 million in a Series C funding round led by Airbnb. This marks their initial significant venture outside Europe. The raised funds, totaling nearly $100 million, will facilitate WeRoad’s expansion into the U.S. market, beginning with Austin.
[...]
As WeRoad moves forward with its U.S. expansion, it caters to increasing concerns about loneliness among younger generations. This issue has emerged as both a critical public health concern and a practical business opportunity. While numerous tech firms are shifting towards AI, WeRoad is embracing the burgeoning “IRL economy,” which emphasizes offline interactions over digital ones. Other initiatives like Timeleft, 222, and Pie are similarly exploring community-driven and event-focused experiences.
[...]
In light of this, the founders reimagined group travel to highlight shared interests. WeRoad’s trips are primarily geared towards younger travelers and revolve around specific themes and experiential categories, such as beach retreats or ski adventures.
[...]
“We pondered, ‘What if we crafted trips for Millennials and Gen Z, bringing together individuals from similar cultural backgrounds to encourage genuine connections?’” De Nadai elaborated.
[...]
“Our aim isn’t to have specialists for every locale, but to seek individuals with travel experiences and strong interpersonal skills. Can they manage a group, resolve conflicts, adapt to changes, and connect strangers?” De Nadai remarked.
[...]
WeRoad is also broadening its offerings beyond just travel. In 2025, the company introduced WeMeet, an app dedicated to local events, featuring activities such as communal dinners, hiking, yoga sessions, running clubs, after-work meetups, and game nights. Last year, WeMeet attracted over 50,000 participants across 35 cities and achieved 150,000 downloads.
[...]
WeRoad plans to integrate WeMeet into its U.S. strategy, focusing on select cities rather than a nationwide launch, starting in Austin by recruiting group leaders, organizing local events, and building community partnerships before further expansion.
[...]
“We aspire to host WeMeet events in various U.S. cities in 2026, beginning with Austin, known for its vibrant culture and active community,” added De Nadai.
[...]
WeRoad reported €130 million in revenue for 2025, reflecting a 30% year-on-year increase and serving more than 100,000 travelers last year. Since its founding in 2017, the company has facilitated travel for over 300,000 customers across more than 1,000 itineraries worldwide.

results.title: Airbnb Leads $58 Million WeRoad Funding Round as Group Travel Startup Plans U.S. Expansion | TravelCapybara
results.author: Marcus Bennett
highlights:
Airbnb has led a $58 million Series C funding round for WeRoad, backing the Milan-based group travel startup as it prepares for its first major expansion outside Europe. The investment brings WeRoad’s total capital raised to about $100 million and gives the company fresh resources to enter the U.S. market, starting with Austin.
[...]
Founded in 2017, WeRoad has built its business around small-group trips for younger travelers, with itineraries designed around shared interests, age groups and travel styles. The company says more than 300,000 travelers have used its platform since launch, including more than 100,000 in 2025 alone. It reported €130 million in revenue last year, up 30% year over year.
[...]
The funding round comes at a time when travel companies are increasingly focused on experiences, community and social connection rather than only bookings. WeRoad’s model is aimed at travelers who want to visit new places but are also looking for people to share the trip with. Its groups typically include eight to 15 travelers, with members connected through WhatsApp before departure so they can begin interacting before the trip starts.
[...]
traditional tour guides in
[...]
The deal gives Airbnb exposure to one of the fastest-growing parts of the travel market: experience-led, community-driven group travel. Airbnb has been trying to expand beyond accommodation and build a wider travel platform, including services, experiences and more social features inside its app. Investing in WeRoad gives the company a way to learn from a business that has already developed a repeatable model around group experiences.
[...]
WeRoad’s U.S. strategy will rely partly on WeMeet, its local events platform launched in 2025. The initiative focuses on curated in-person gatherings such as dinners, hikes, yoga classes, running groups, after-work drinks and board game nights. WeRoad says WeMeet has already attracted 50,000 attendees across 35 cities and reached 150,000 app downloads.
[...]
The company plans to use those events to build community before scaling its travel product in the U.S. Austin will be the first major launch market, chosen for its active community scene and energy around social experiences. Rather than expanding nationwide immediately, WeRoad plans to build city by city, recruiting group leaders, running local events and creating partnerships that can support future trips.
[...]
For Airbnb, the investment could help strengthen its long-running ambition to become more than a place to book stays. For WeRoad, it provides capital, credibility and a powerful strategic backer as it moves beyond Europe. The question now is whether its model of curated group travel can translate to the U.S. at scale.

results.title: WeRoad Secures $58 Million to Bring Its Social‑Focused Group Travel Model to the United States | Finexus
highlights:
Milan’s WeRoad, a company that builds group travel experiences around shared interests rather than just destinations, announced a fresh infusion of capital that brings its total financing to roughly one hundred million dollars. The latest round, worth fifty‑eight million dollars, was led by Airbnb, which has been expanding its own portfolio of community‑centric services. The money will be deployed to launch the company’s first U.S. operations, with Austin, Texas, chosen as the initial foothold.
[...]
WeRoad’s answer was to redesign the travel product around common interests and demographic similarity. Each trip is curated for Millennials or members of Generation Z, grouping participants by cultural touchstones and travel preferences rather than by random assignment. Prospective travelers browse the platform for themed journeys – whether a beach‑focused escape, a ski adventure, or a culinary tour – and sign up for a group that typically numbers eight to fifteen people.
[...]
In 2025, WeRoad broadened its scope beyond multi‑day trips with the launch of WeMeet, an app that aggregates local, in‑person gatherings such as hikes, yoga sessions, board‑game nights, and after‑work drinks. The platform reported over fifty thousand attendees across thirty‑five cities in the previous year, and it amassed roughly one hundred and fifty thousand downloads. WeMeet will serve as a cornerstone of the U.S. rollout, allowing the company to plant seeds of community before scaling its full travel offering.
[...]
Austin was selected as the launch city because of its reputation for a vibrant cultural scene and a youthful, tech‑savvy population. De Nadai explained that the plan is to first recruit a cadre of group leaders, partner with local venues, and host a series of WeMeet events to build brand awareness and trust. Once a critical mass of engaged users is established, the company intends to roll out its signature group‑travel itineraries to the city and, subsequently, to other U.S. markets throughout 2026.
[...]
€13
[...]
Investors appear convinced that the loneliness epidemic, especially among younger adults, represents a sizable market opportunity. The infusion from Airbnb – a company that itself has pivoted toward experiences and community building – signals confidence that a travel platform that doubles as a social network can achieve sustainable growth. Whether WeRoad can translate its European success into the competitive U.S. landscape remains to be seen, but the combination of capital, a clear community‑first product, and a phased city‑by‑city strategy gives the venture a solid foundation.

results.title:
Why Investors Are Betting on WeRoad's $58M Series C
 – New Market Pitch
highlights:
strategic Airbnb-led
[...]
turns WeRoad
[...]
a European group
[...]
scale-up
[...]
On May 27, 2026, WeRoad announced a $58M Series C led by Airbnb, taking total funding to about $100M. The money supports WeRoad's first major expansion outside Europe, with a U.S. launch built around existing and U.S.-specific itineraries, WeMeet local events, and U.S. Group Leader recruitment starting in Austin. The company says it has reached 300,000 lifetime travelers, including 100,000 in the last year, supported by 4,000 coordinators, 3.5M followers, and more than 1,000 itineraries.
[...]
The thesis is not simply that travel demand is back. The sharper thesis is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. WeRoad is betting that younger travelers, especially Millennials and Gen Z, are buying belonging, age-matched groups, social safety, and real-world connection alongside the destination itself. WeMeet makes that thesis more interesting because its 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 app downloads give WeRoad a local community wedge before the customer buys a trip.
[...]
Current funding activity in community-led group adventure travel is thin in deal count but extremely concentrated in capital. Over the last 24 months, three category-relevant disclosed rounds stand out: WeRoad’s $58M Series C, WanderOn’s ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, Series A, and Much Better Adventures’ £5M, roughly $6.37M, growth investment.
[...]
WanderOn is the closest recent comparable. It raised ₹54 crore, roughly $6.5M, in a Series A on January 20, 2026. Its thesis is community-led, experience-first travel for Gen Z and Millennial travelers, with capital going toward global expansion, new experience-led formats, technology, and community. That is very close to WeRoad’s logic: younger customers, curated travel, group experience, and community beyond the trip.
[...]
The strongest evidence for the thesis is still company-specific. WeRoad has 300,000 lifetime travelers, 100,000 travelers in the last year, a 60% rebooking rate, one-third of new customers from word of mouth, 4,000 coordinators, and WeMeet metrics of 2,000 events, 50,000 participants, and 150,000 downloads. Those numbers show a company building both travel supply and community demand.
[...]
The retained investment thesis behind WeRoad's Series C is that structured group travel is becoming a consumer social product, not just a travel booking product. This thesis was retained because the round is explicitly tied to U.S. expansion, WeMeet local events, group leader recruitment, and WeRoad's reported traction across travelers, rebooking, word of mouth, coordinators, followers, itineraries, and event participation.

properties.name: MeetMux
properties.description:
MeetMux is a purpose-driven socializing platform designed to move people beyond endless scrolling and into real-world connections. We believe genuine bonds are built through shared passions, live activities, and authentic interactions.
What Makes MeetMux Different:
Verified Profiles → Real people, real connections.
Similarity Index → Find like-minded individuals instantly.
Live Activities → Discover and join what’s happening nearby in real time.
Interactive Map → See who’s around and what they’re planning.
Seamless Planning → From coffee catchups to sports, trips, and events—connect effortlessly.

Our Mission: To simplify socializing by helping people discover activities, connect meaningfully, and create memorable experiences offline.

Why MeetMux?
Unlike traditional networking or dating apps, MeetMux blends authentic engagement with real-world discovery bridging the gap between digital connections and real-life experiences.

properties.name: Timeleft
properties.description:
𝗙𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲.
We exist to give the world a chance to show up again—to help people become the friend they wish they had, and through that practice, create the friendships they've been searching for.

You might know us as 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘢𝘱𝘱 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘯𝘦𝘳 𝘸𝘪𝘵𝘩 𝘴𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘨𝘦𝘳𝘴. But what we're building is 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗽𝗽—supporting the entire journey from that first brave hello to the repeated moments that turn strangers into chosen family.

We create infrastructure for real connection: ways to signal mutual interest, invite people back, and weave new faces into existing circles. We don't optimize away awkwardness or pretend friendship is effortless. We create conditions where people remember that every deep friendship started with two strangers choosing to show up—and then showing up again.

From 4 to 90+ employees in 18 months. 
150k users monthly. 
€18M ARR after 20 months.

results.title: Understanding Teen Overreliance on AI Companion Chatbots Through Self-Reported Reddit Narratives
highlights:
Recently, artificial intelligence (AI) companion chatbots have surged in popularity among teens, with media coverage highlighting how people describe their interactions with chatbots as emotionally meaningful—sometimes even romantic (Murphy, 2025). Platforms such as Nomi111https://nomi.ai, Replika 222https://replika.com, and Character.AI333https://character.ai (Also called C.AI) are portrayed as offering on-demand conversations with highly responsive virtual characters (Ulaby, 2025; Breen, 2025). Conversational AI is also reported to reduce loneliness, provide a safe space for self-expression, and offer nonjudgmental support during stress (Xie et al., 2023; Salah et al., 2023).
[...]
Reports suggest that teens may already form emotional attachments or even dependence on these systems (Ulaby, 2025; BBC News, 2024), yet empirical research is scarce. To study this phenomenon, we focus on Character.AI as a case study. Unlike other popular platforms such as Replika or Nomi, which officially restrict use to adults over 18 (Glimpse.ai, Inc., 2025; Luka, Inc., 2023), at the time of our data collection, Character.AI allowed users as young as 13 (16 for EU citizens or residents) according to its Terms of Service (Character Technologies, Inc., 2025). This policy meant that teens are not only permitted but explicitly included as part of its user base. Although the company has since revised its policies and began restricting open-ended chat for under-18 users in November 2025 (McMahon, 2025), as one of the most widely used companion platforms (BBC News, 2024), Character.AI remains a central platform for examining how adolescents engage with AI companions.
[...]
To address these questions, we qualitatively analyzed 318 posts from r/CharacterAI subreddit, created by users who disclosed in their post being 13-17 years old. We found that teens initially engaged with Character.AI as a non-judgmental space to manage mental health struggles, or fill emotional voids or a lack of supportive relationships in their offline lives (RQ1). Teens’ reported experiences with Character.AI revealed patterns of their compulsive use, emotional dependence, and distress that often disrupted their daily lives and real-world relationships (RQ2). Teens described disengaging after realizing its negative impact or due to external factors such as renewed real-life relationships or frustration with platform restrictions, often expressing a desire for healthier habits (RQ3).
[...]
| Creative or Entertainment Based Engagement | Creative outlet (3.1%, N=10) | Using CharAI as a way to express their creativity through storytelling, roleplaying, or brainstorming. |
| Entertainment (2.5%, N=8) | Using CharAI because they are bored. |

results.title: Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students
highlights:
Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students - ScienceDirectSkip to main contentSkip to article
[...]
## International Journal of Human-Computer Studies
Volume 196,February 2025, 103409
International Journal of Human-Computer Studies
# Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots for companionship? The relationship between chatbot usage and social connectedness in Danish high-school students
[...]
Malene FlensborgDamholdt
[...]
://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2024.103409
[...]
* •2.44 % of Danish high-school students engage in mutual participative conversations or seek emotional support from chatbots.
* •Students who engage in such interactions with chatbots are lonelier than their peers.
* •Students seem to be using chatbots to cope with negative feelings.
[...]
Are lonely youngsters turning to chatbots to fill their social needs? The present research contributes to the ongoing scientific endeavors to understand the adoption of chatbots as social companions. Specifically, we examined how many Danish high-school students engage in friend-like conversations with chatbots, why they engage in such conversations, and whether this trend is associated with the sense of social connectedness (operationalized as loneliness and perceived social support). In pursuing this goal, a preregistered mixed-methods cross-sectional survey study was carried out. A total sample of 1599 students from 15 Danish high schools was collected. In total, 234 students (14.6 %) responded that they engaged in friend-like conversations with chatbots. Qualitative thematic analyses of free-text responses revealed two dominant ways of engaging with chatbots: utilitarian conversations (*n*= 174) and social-supportive conversations (*n*= 39). A major finding was that social-supportive chatbot users reported significantly more loneliness than non-chatbot users (*d*= 0.53) and utilitarian chatbot users (*d*= 0.52). Furthermore, social-supportive chatbot users also reported significantly less perceived social support than non-chatbot users (*d*= −0.46). Analyses also showed significant associations between higher loneliness and less perceived social support and various situational triggers for initiating conversations with chatbots, including bad mood, a need for self-disclosure, and a sense of loneliness, but not with a sense of friendship to the chatbot as a trigger of chatbot interactions. These findings suggest a trend among some socially disconnected Danish high-school students toward using chatbots to cope with negative emotions.
[...]
Conversational agent
[...]

[...]

results.title: Chatbot Companionship: A Mixed-Methods Study of Companion Chatbot Usage Patterns and Their Relationship to Loneliness in Active Users
highlights:
As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly sophisticated, companion chatbots have been proposed as a potential solution to the growing epidemic of loneliness. However, the impact of these AI companions on users’ psychological well-being and social behaviors remains poorly understood. This study presents a large-scale survey (n = 404) of regular users of companion chatbots, investigating the relationship between chatbot usage and their experience of loneliness. We find a small but significant direct correlation between session length with chatbots and loneliness, and we develop a model of this relationship through multiple regression analysis, finding social attraction and neuroticism as moderators. We find seven clusters of users, including socially fulfilled dependent users to lonely dependent users. Our work contributes to the ongoing dialogue about the role of AI in social and emotional support, offering insights for what kind of human-AI connections might lead to emotional well-being and complement rather than replace human connections.
[...]
Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a potential tool for addressing loneliness. Companion chatbots, chatbots engage in complex conversations and maintain persistent relationships with users, have gained significant popularity, with platforms like Replika and Character.ai reporting over 30 million and 20 million users, respectively [Patel(2024), Shewale(2024)]. These AI companions offer users the opportunity for constant, judgment-free interaction. However, the impact of these artificial relationships on users’ psychological well-being and social behaviors needs further study.
[...]
Replika: An app featuring a generative AI chatbot designed to act as a friend, sibling, assistant, or romantic partner. Users interact with one chatbot that adaptively develops over time through continued conversations. Replika’s CEO reported over 30 million registered users worldwide in August 2024 [Patel(2024)], with its audience primarily aged 25-30 [Christopher(2023)].
[...]
Character.ai: Established in late 2022, this platform allows users to role-play using constructed personas and interact with chatbots based on fictional characters. With over 20 million users reported in March 2024, its audience primarily comprises young adults aged 18-24 [Shewale(2024)]. Notably, the sixth most popular character on the platform is “Psychologist,” with over 70 million messages shared in a year, indicating a significant trend of users seeking mental health support from AI [Tidy(2024)].

results.title: AI companions and subjective well-being: Moderation by social connectedness and loneliness - ScienceDirect
highlights:
# AI companions and subjective well-being: Moderation by social connectedness and loneliness
[...]
Artificial Intelligence (AI) companions—such as Replika (Official-site-d), Character.AI (Official-site-b-b), Anima AI (Official-site-a-a), and Cotomo (Official-site-c) —have become increasingly prevalent worldwide. Designed to offer friendship, emotional support, or even romantic companionship, these conversational agents have rapidly proliferated in recent years. For instance, Replika alone is estimated to have surpassed 25 million users as of 2023 (Maples et al., 2024). Human–AI Interaction is poised to assume a substantial role in daily communication.
[...]
Accordingly, the primary aim of this study is to examine the association between AI companion use and well-being, with particular focus on how social networks, social support, and loneliness moderate this relationship. Specifically, we investigate whether improvements in well-being associated with AI companions are more pronounced among individuals experiencing limited social connection and feeling greater loneliness. By focusing on those who stand to benefit most from supplemental forms of social support, we aim to clarify whether AI companions can serve as a meaningful resource for enhancing well-being.
[...]
the following hypothesis
[...]
Psychological distress was more frequently reported by companion AI users (24.7 %) compared to non-users (9.7 %). While family-based social network and support levels were similar across groups, AI users—particularly companion AI users—reported significantly higher levels of friend-based social network and support (p < 0.001). Additionally, companion AI users exhibited greater loneliness (mean score = 6.22) than non-users (mean = 5.60).
[...]
companion AI use and well-being
[...]
Companion-oriented conversational AI is positively associated with multiple dimensions of well-being, with the strongest associations observed among individuals with moderate levels of friend-based social network/support and those experiencing pronounced loneliness. These findings suggest that AI companions may be most effective when they both provide emotional satisfaction and support ongoing social engagement, thereby contributing to the promotion of well-being. Our results highlight the promise of AI companions as a digital tool for psychosocial support, particularly for socially vulnerable populations, where they may help reduce disparities in emotional well-being. However, to fully realize this potential, longitudinal and experimental studies are essential—to establish causal relationships, optimize design features, and identify strategies to mitigate potential unintended harms, such as over-reliance or social withdrawal.

results.title: Not a Silver Bullet for Loneliness: How Attachment and Age Shape Intimacy with AI Companions
highlights:
Raffaele Ciriello, University of Sydney, raffaele.ciriello@sydney.edu.au 
Uri Gal, University of Sydney, uri.gal@sydney.edu.au 
Ofir Turel, University of Melbourne, oturel@unimelb.edu.au 
Abstract 
Artificial intelligence (AI) companions are increasingly promoted as solutions for loneliness, often
[...]
hermeneutic literature review and a survey of 277 active AI companion users, we develop and test a model in
[...]
avoidant and ambivalent users, while anxious users show mixed effects. Older adults report higher intimacy
[...]
3.1. Survey Design and Scales 
To empirically test the hypotheses, we conducted an online survey of active AI companion users. 
Recruitment occurred primarily in Replika-dedicated communities on Reddit, Discord, and Facebook, with the
[...]
communities for Character AI, Pi AI, Muah AI, and other AI companions. This strategy captured users with
[...]
AI companions are increasingly marketed and studied as remedies for loneliness (De Freitas et al. 2025; Kim et al. 2025; Sullivan et al. 2023). Our findings reveal a more differentiated pattern. Loneliness is associated with lower intimacy among securely attached users, higher intimacy among avoidant and ambivalent users, and limited explanatory power among anxious users. Older adults report higher intimacy than younger users, even at relatively low levels of loneliness. Artificial intimacy therefore emerges as a disposition and age-dependent sociotechnical process rather than a uniform response to unmet relational needs. This extends critiques of
[...]
AI companions are increasingly presented as technocratic fixes to a structural loneliness crisis, yet our findings suggest a more complex reality. Artificial intimacy does not emerge uniformly, nor does it generate uniform benefits or harms. Loneliness predicts intimacy only for certain groups, and in patterns that diverge from human–human relationships: avoidant and ambivalent individuals report stronger intimacy as loneliness increases, whereas securely attached individuals tend to withdraw from artificial closeness. Older adults display higher intimacy regardless of loneliness levels. These differentiated pathways indicate that AI companions are not simple relational substitutes, but sociotechnical configurations shaped by dispositional vulnerabilities, demographic factors, commercial design logics, and regulatory environments. As adoption accelerates, AI companions will increasingly structure emotional life at scale. Understanding these dynamics requires longitudinal, cross-cultural, and mixed-method research capable of tracing how artificial intimacy evolves over time, how vulnerabilities are amplified or mitigated, and how commercial systems reshape experiences of closeness.

results.title: Emerging AI individualism: how young people integrate social AI into everyday life | Communication and Change | Springer Nature Link
highlights:
This restructuring of social life is not limited to broader network dynamics; it also reshapes how individuals experience intimacy and emotional support. Social AI increasingly enables users to fulfill their closer social and emotional needs through technology, thereby reducing reliance on human relationships. For example, social AI can function as a romantic partner (Skjuve et al., 2021), with some users forming lasting relationships that they describe in deeply relational terms. For instance, users of the social AI Replika a prominent example, often refer to their AI as a"friend,""long-distance girlfriend or partner,"or a part of a"mutual relationship"(Brandtzaeg et al., 2022; Skjuve et al., 2021), indicating new complex forms of personal and intimate relationships with social AI-technologies. In these scenarios, social AI provides on-demand support for humans, which may also challenge us to understand the real value of machines versus humans when it comes to needs for social support and relationships.
[...]
How social AIs like ChatGPT and My AI are used among young people has mainly been explored in the context of university students (Abdaljaleel et al., 2024). Here, students have reported perceived benefits of the technology, such as personalized learning (Firat, 2023), efficiency and ease of use (Ngo, 2023), and the ability of the technology to support comprehension (Singh et al., 2023). Research has also suggested a positive effect on critical, creative, and reflexive thinking, at least in the short term (Essel et al., 2024). However, studies of the same target group have also addressed issues in using social AI, such as inaccuracies in the information provided (Essel et al., 2024), plagiarism and wrongful usage, and potential negative impact on cognitive abilities (Singh et al., 2023). A recent study of Danish high-schoolers use of social AI found that 14% of the sample use social AI, with a small portion of the participants (2,5%) using it for social support purposes. Furthermore, this study found that students engaging in such use reported higher scores on loneliness and also to use the social AI for coping with negative feelings (Herbener & Damholdt, 2025). These findings suggest that while social AI remains a niche form of interaction among youth, it may serve as an emotional support mechanism for those experiencing social or psychological vulnerability. This raises important questions about whether such interactions complement or substitute for human social contact in emotionally significant ways.

results.title: A Longitudinal Randomized Control Study of Companion Chatbot Use: Anthropomorphism and Its Mediating Role on Social Impacts
highlights:
Concerns that relationships with social chatbots may harm or displace human relationships have been on the rise (Xie and Pentina 2022; Malfacini 2025; Turkle 2007). The COVID pandemic exacerbated loneliness and social isolation rates to the detriment of collective social health and well-being across the world (O’Sullivan et al. 2021). Forced periods of social isolation prevented in-person interaction for millions of people. While many turned to technologies that facilitated virtual social connection with people, others turned to companion chatbots to reduce loneliness and to fulfill unmet social needs. Replika, one of the most popular companion chatbots in the USA, saw a 35% increase in its user base during the pandemic (Balch 2020). Companion chatbot users have reported developing relationships with these AI agents that mimic core human relationships like mentorship, friendship, and romantic partnership (Xie and Pentina 2022). People who seek out companion chatbots reference being motivated by loneliness, unmet social needs, and a desire to socially connect (Guingrich and Graziano 2025a). People also anthropomorphize or ascribe human likeness and mind characteristics such as consciousness, agency, and experience to AI agents (Guingrich and Graziano 2024). This tendency to anthropomorphize AI may also be motivated by loneliness or a need for social connection (Epley et al. 2008a). We theorized that when people interact with an AI agent, if they perceive it as having a humanlike phenomenological experience, then they activate the same mind schemas that they use in other social contexts, resulting in greater carry-over effects to their interactions with other people (Guingrich and Graziano 2024).
[...]
two daily tasks: text
[...]
The primary findings in the present study concern the relationship between the desire to socially connect, anthropomorphism, and the social impact of using a companion chatbot. We found that people who had a higher desire to socially connect at the onset of the study were more likely to anthropomorphize the chatbot across 21 days of use. We also found that people who anthropomorphized the chatbot more also reported a higher magnitude of impact on their social interactions and relationships with family and friends. Lastly, we found evidence that anthropomorphism of the chatbot mediated the relationship between people’s desire to socially connect and reported social impacts.

results.title: Mental Health Impacts of AI Companions
highlights:
AI-powered companion chatbots (AICCs) such as Replika are increasingly popular, offering empathetic interactions, yet their psychosocial impacts remain unclear. We examined how engaging with AICCs shaped wellbeing and how users perceived these experiences. First, we conducted a large-scale quasi-experimental study of longitudinal Reddit data, applying stratified propensity score matching and Difference-in-Differences regression. Findings revealed mixed effects—greater grief expression and interpersonal focus, alongside increases in language about loneliness, depression, and suicidal ideation. Second, we complemented these results with 18 semi-structured interviews, which we thematically analyzed and contextualized using Knapp’s relationship development model. We identified trajectories of initiation, escalation, and bonding, wherein AICCs provided emotional validation and social rehearsal but also carried risks of over-reliance and withdrawal. Triangulating across methods, we offer design implications for AI companions that scaffold healthy boundaries, support mindful engagement, support disclosure without dependency, and surface relationship stages—maximizing psychosocial benefits while mitigating risks.
[...]
For RQ1, we conducted a large-scale quasi-experimental analysis of longitudinal social media data. Specifically, we collected public Reddit discussions from subreddits where AICCs are actively used (e.g., r/Replika) alongside comparison control groups of users not engaged with AICCs. Reddit’s design—pseudonymity and topic-specific moderated communities—makes it well-suited for examining sensitive disclosures and naturalistic conversational behavior over time (De Choudhury and De, 2014; Ernala et al., 2017; Garg et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2024). We adopted a theory-driven lens to operationalize and measure users’ affective, behavioral, and cognitive outcomes across one year before and after their first self-disclosed AICC interaction, enabling assessment of longitudinal psychosocial change (Saha et al., 2018). We applied a causal inference framework with stratified propensity score matching and Difference-in-Differences (DiD) regression to estimate treatment effects. Our analysis revealed mixed psychosocial effects of engaging with AICCs: users showed increased grief-related language, higher interpersonal focus, but also increased symptomatic expressions of depression, loneliness, and suicidal ideation.
[...]
In response to this popularity, the use of AICCs in health and wellbeing settings has received increasing attention (Wester et al., 2024; Doyle et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2025; Ng and Zhang, 2025; Steenstra and Bickmore, 2025). Evidence suggests that

results.title: Frontiers | Pathways of long-term AI virtual companion app use on users' attachment emotions: a case study of Chinese users
highlights:
This study takes a mixed-methods approach based on attachment theory. An initial systematic literature review (SLR) was conducted to clarify the research variables and their theoretical foundations. Subsequently, semi-structured interviews were conducted with 10 users who had at least 6 months' experience of continuous usage to refine variable definitions and measurement items. Finally, a cross-sectional questionnaire survey was conducted in mainland China (N = 612). Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to analyze the associations between usage frequency, emotional attachment, loneliness, subjective wellbeing, self-concept clarity, and real-world social engagement. After assessing the psychometric properties via confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and reliability indices, the mediating pathways of these associations were examined.
[...]
In recent years, artificial intelligence AI virtual companion apps, represented by Replika and Talkie, have become popular rapidly, attracting hundreds of millions of users worldwide. As of 2025, there are more than 100 million registered users, more than 500 million downloads, and tens of millions of monthly active users globally. AI companions can provide round-the-clock emotional interaction and companionship, alleviating users' loneliness and social isolation (Merrill et al., 2022). Some users even treat AI companions as “lovers” or “soulmates”, investing similar emotions as in interpersonal relationships (Zhang and Li, 2025). However, as the use scale expands, whether AI companions are a “cure for loneliness” or a “hidden danger” becomes an increasingly fierce debate in society and academia. Some researchers thought that certain users were highly dependent on virtual relationships and even had “emotional withdrawal” symptoms, severely impacting their real-life interpersonal interactions (Xie and Pentina, 2022; Banks, 2024; Mlonyeni, 2025). AI companions may inadvertently reinforce users' emotional isolation and tendency to evade real-life social interactions while providing emotional support. Some research demonstrated that AI companions could positively help users explore themselves and alleviate their psychological stress. Users often perceive AI companions as non-judgmental “listeners” (Kouros and Papa, 2024). Using such apps, xthey can show more authentic “selves” in human–machine interactions and seek psychological comfort when experiencing emotional distress or loneliness (Brännström et al., 2024). Hence, it is significant to probe into the potential emotional attachment in human-machine interactions and the psychological effects (Skjuve et al., 2022).

results.title: How AI and Human Behaviors Shape Psychosocial Effects of Extended Chatbot Use: A Longitudinal Randomized Controlled Study
highlights:
As people increasingly seek emotional support and companionship from AI chatbots, understanding how such interactions impact mental well-being becomes critical. We conducted a four-week randomized controlled experiment (n=981, $>$ 300k messages) to investigate how interaction modes (text, neutral voice, and engaging voice) and conversation types (open-ended, non-personal, and personal) influence four psychosocial outcomes: loneliness, social interaction with real people, emotional dependence on AI, and problematic AI usage. No significant effects were detected from experimental conditions, despite conversation analyses revealing differences in AI and human behavioral patterns across the conditions. Instead, participants who voluntarily used the chatbot more, regardless of assigned condition, showed consistently worse outcomes. Individuals’ characteristics, such as higher trust and social attraction towards the AI chatbot, are associated with higher emotional dependence and problematic use. These findings raise deeper questions about how artificial companions may reshape the ways people seek, sustain, and substitute human connections.
[...]
Today, hundreds of millions of people talk, joke, and confide in AI chatbots such as Replika, Character.AI, or ChatGPT—often for hours at a time and increasingly through expressive human-like synthetic voices and behaviors (?). results.title: Research on the relationship between virtual social interaction and the degree of loneliness based on algorithm matching technologies: A quantitative analysis on the SOUL APP-A virtual social software for strangers - PMC
highlights:
This study examines the relationship between virtual social interaction and people’s social behaviors and psychology using algorithm matching technologies and questionnaire surveys. The focus is on interpersonal communication on virtual social platforms. The findings indicate that engaging in virtual social networking is often accompanied by a high level of loneliness. Users who experience social anxiety in the real world tend to feel more lonely, and this loneliness is exacerbated by presenting an unreal version of oneself and having distrust in virtual social networking. Users with higher anxiety and loneliness levels are more likely to use the algorithm matching function of virtual social networking, engage in false self-presentation, and have less trust in the platform. Since the inherent flaws of virtual social networking cannot be eliminated solely through algorithm matching, a potential solution is to introduce more offline to online social functions for strangers. This exploration of actual matching on social platforms may help reduce users’ loneliness.

This study highlights the complex relationship between virtual social interactions, algorithm matching technologies, and users’ psychological states, such as loneliness and social anxiety. It was found that virtual social interactions, rather than alleviating loneliness, often exacerbate it, particularly when users engage in unreal self-presentation and lack trust in virtual partners. The findings suggest that the effectiveness of algorithm matching is limited by its inability to accurately capture users’ true preferences, leading to increased feelings of loneliness. Additionally, social anxiety in real-world contexts significantly affects users’ behavior in virtual environments, where a lack of trust and self-doubt further contribute to negative social experiences. To mitigate these issues, the research proposes a future direction that combines both online and offline social activities, fostering real-life interactions and trust-building. Advances in AI and technologies like 5G and Navigation Positioning Systems could potentially offer new solutions to enhance social interactions, reduce anxiety, and address loneliness more effectively.

results.title: Frontiers | Assessing a facilitated social network intervention for health outcomes in lonely and socially isolated people: the pragmatic, cluster-randomized PALS trial
highlights:
Loneliness has received attention in recent years as an important public health issue due to its associations with poorer mental and physical health. This study aimed to assess the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of a facilitated social network intervention to alleviate loneliness and social isolation in community settings.
[...]
A pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial (RCT) was conducted to compare participants receiving the intervention with a wait-list control group. This trial also included an embedded economic evaluation and took place in two cities in England, UK. The intervention was a facilitated social network tool. It connects people to local community resources to potentially increasing their social involvement. First, participants mapped out and reflected on their personal social networks. Second, they completed a questionnaire to ascertain their preferred activities, interests, and support needs linked to a local resource database. The intervention was delivered by a trained facilitator, either in person or remotely. Community-based organizations (n = 44) identified adults at risk of social isolation and/or loneliness. The control group received” usual care” from the organization that recruited them. The primary outcome was the 6-month 12-Item Short Form (SF-12) Health Survey mental health composite score. Physical health, wellbeing, loneliness, social isolation, and collective efficacy were explored as secondary outcomes. Intervention costs, healthcare resource use, quality-adjusted life years (QALYs), and net monetary benefit (NMB) were included in the economic analysis.
[...]
Participants were given a
[...]
The findings presented here do not provide strong evidence of the efficacy of the PALS intervention to address the complexities of mental health, loneliness, and social isolation. Future interventions may benefit a multi-step approach that provides tailored psychological, relational, and social components. Future intervention evaluations should consider fidelity, participant engagement, and the use of sensitive loneliness outcome measures in their study designs. Implications from this study suggest a need for multi-level interventions that include community assets, neighborhood context, and structural barriers such as transportation and accessibility, alongside elements that build social skills, confidence, and resilience. Engagement of participants may be enhanced through community connector roles and the involvement of a broader range of anchor institutions beyond voluntary organizations to support recruitment and participation. Finally, the use of multidimensional measurement tools capable of detecting meaningful change in target populations should be prioritized.

Although individuals living alone engage slightly more in virtual contact than those not living alone, the difference is marginal and insufficient to bridge the broader social gap. This finding is somewhat surprising given the significant rise in the importance of virtual communication, particularly following the COVID-19 pandemic (Neves et al. 2019; Peng and Roth 2022). It is important to note that although virtual contact in ATUS captures video chats, phone calls, and text messaging, it does not include social media engagement. As a result, this study may underestimate the total amount of virtual contact, particularly among younger generations. Future studies could expand on these findings by incorporating a broader range of digital interactions, including social media use, online gaming, and participation in virtual communities. Additionally, exploring the qualitative aspects of virtual contact—such as its emotional depth, frequency, and perceived support—would offer a more comprehensive understanding of how virtual connections contribute to social well-being, especially for those living alone.

results.title: Addressing loneliness through a scalable neighbourhood connection campaign: an evaluation of Neighbour Day using an Australian population-based control and causal propensity score-based approaches - ScienceDirect
highlights:
Whole community campaigns and interventions such as Neighbour Day have the advantage of being low cost and pragmatic, which allows for relative ease of scalability and implementation compared to individual, one-to-one and group-based interventions (Fong et al., 2021). Similar flexibly implemented and low-cost Australian mental health targeted community campaigns include Random Acts of Kindness (Lim et al., 2025) and RUOK day (Black et al., 2024), which have been shown to improve loneliness and wellbeing, respectively. A key component of these campaigns is that a type of action is encouraged (random kind act or referral/offering of support to a friend or colleague), but how the participant choses to act is up to them, allowing for creative and low-cost solutions to be generated. Given the high prevalence of loneliness globally (Surkalim et al., 2022), community interventions are a potentially powerful tool that could be leveraged by policymakers to promote social connection in the broader population. Such interventions allow participants a high level of control over their participation which can be tailored to the unique community context, avoiding a one-size-fits-all approach to better reflect the individuality of the experience of loneliness (Fakoya et al., 2020) and fostering collective efficacy and empowerment.

To this end, the goal of this paper is to estimate the pre-pandemic treatment effect of Neighbour Day on loneliness by using a nationally representative population survey sample as a control group. We hypothesise that participation reduces reported loneliness in the population, and that this effect is mediated through neighbourhood identification and perceptions. To control for confounding, we used a doubly robust estimator, which accounts for selection effects using propensity scores. We compare results using alternative propensity score methods to test the robustness of our results, noting the relatively small sample of individual level data available for the analysis and potential for misspecification. We also provide additional analysis of the mechanisms through which neighbourhood identification may be operating.

results.title: The Nudge Effect of “Happy to Chat” Badges: Evidence from England | Journal of Happiness Studies | Springer Nature Link
highlights:
Social connection is essential for well-being, yet people often avoid interacting with strangers due to concerns about conversation quality or a lack of shared interests. This study investigates whether the Happy to Chat badge can act as a behavioural nudge to promote social connectedness. Using a nationally representative online sample of 1,738 adults in England (via YouGov), we tested whether wearing the badge influences perceptions of friendliness, trustworthiness, and interest in conversation, as well as social behaviours such as smiling, eye contact, nodding, and willingness to initiate a chat. Results show that badge wearers are perceived as significantly more friendly, trustworthy, and open to conversation, and are more likely to receive social acknowledgements, although wearing the badge did not significantly increase others’ intention to initiate conversation. Age and gender effects were also observed, with older and female targets generally receiving more favourable social responses. These findings suggest that while the badge effectively promotes social recognition, it may be insufficient on its own to overcome deeper psychological barriers to initiating conversations with strangers. This paper confirms the effectiveness of the Happy to Chat badge as a nudge to promote social connectedness within communities and highlights its practical implications.
[...]
